title: Floating to an Unfamiliar Unit: Accepting a Safe Assignment
subtitle: A med-surg RN is floated to a busy telemetry unit. The nurse must accept the assignment safely — communicating competencies, getting oriented, taking patients and tasks within scope, and getting help for unfamiliar ones — without committing patient abandonment.

patient.name: Float Assignment
patient.admitDate: Med-surg RN floated to a telemetry unit for the shift. Must accept a safe assignment, work within competence, and get help for unfamiliar tasks — without abandoning patients.

vitals.temp: Floating is a legal staffing practice
Tell charge nurse your skills + limits

labs.r: Unfamiliarity alone isn't grounds to refuse.
Find supplies before you need them.

nursesNote: A med-surg RN is floated to a telemetry unit. Floating is a legal, common staffing practice; an assignment cannot be refused for unfamiliarity alone. The nurse reports to the unit, notifies the charge nurse of clinical background and any limitations, and requests a brief orientation (layout, equipment, emergency supplies, codes, where to get help). The nurse accepts the more stable patients and tasks within competence, and asks that unfamiliar high-risk tasks — such as titrating a vasoactive drip the nurse has never managed — be reassigned or performed with help. A buddy-system pairing and the chain of command support safe care; concerns are documented through proper channels. The nurse keeps patient safety first and does not abandon patients: declining must happen before accepting report, and walking off after taking report constitutes patient abandonment.
providerOrders: Report to the receiving unit; notify the charge nurse of background and limitations
Obtain a brief orientation to layout, equipment, emergency supplies, and codes
Accept stable patients and tasks within competence
Request help or reassignment for unfamiliar high-risk tasks (e.g., unfamiliar drips)
Provide safe care, use the buddy system, and raise concerns via the chain of command

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does a safe float require?

opts.text: Floating is a legal staffing practice; an assignment can't be refused for unfamiliarity alone
The float nurse should tell the charge nurse their competencies and limitations
A brief orientation to the unit, equipment, and emergency supplies is appropriate
A float nurse should perform any task assigned, even one they've never been trained to do
Leaving the unit after accepting report because it's unfamiliar is patient abandonment
Patient safety is the priority guiding which patients and tasks to accept
The float nurse should be assigned the more stable patients and familiar tasks
Unfamiliar high-risk tasks should be reassigned or done with help, not attempted alone
A buddy-system pairing with a unit nurse supports safe care
Concerns about an unsafe assignment go up the chain of command and are documented
Declining a task outside competence must happen AFTER taking report on all patients
Saying nothing about limitations and hoping for the best is acceptable

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps when you arrive floated

opts.text: Notify the charge nurse of your background and any limitations
Request a brief orientation to the unit, equipment, and emergency supplies
Accept stable patients and tasks within your competence
Ask that unfamiliar high-risk tasks be reassigned or done with help
Provide safe care, using the buddy system and chain of command for concerns

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the action support a safe float?

opts.text: Tell the charge nurse your competencies and limitations
Request orientation to the unfamiliar unit and its equipment
Accept the more stable patients and familiar tasks
Ask for help or reassignment of an unfamiliar high-risk task
Use the buddy system and ask questions when unsure
Leave the hospital after taking report because it's not your specialty
Independently titrate a vasoactive drip you've never managed
Say nothing about your limitations and hope it works out

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Report to the unit and tell the charge nurse your background and limits
Get oriented to layout, equipment, codes, and where to find help
Take the more stable patients and tasks within your competence
Hand off or get help with tasks outside your competence
Document concerns via the chain of command while continuing safe care
The float nurse communicates background and requests orientation
Takes the stable patients and asks for help with unfamiliar equipment
Requests reassignment of a task outside competence
Documents concerns through the chain of command and keeps caring for patients
Leaves the unit after accepting report because it's 'not my specialty'
Performs an unfamiliar high-risk procedure alone instead of asking for help
Refuses to tell the charge nurse about any limitations
Manages an unstable drip without training, risking the patient
